document_title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
notes: No welfare-adjacent capability claims. The paper discusses model capabilities (harmlessness, helpfulness, chain-of-thought reasoning) purely in terms of task performance.

The document mentions self-preservation and deception as emergent capabilities but does not frame these as welfare-adjacent. Less CWG than later documents because capability language is more abstract and less relational.

document_title: Model Card and Evaluations for Claude Models
framing: personality, steerable, conversational
excerpt: They can take direction on tone and 'personality,' and users have described them as feeling steerable and conversational.
notes: Same capability-welfare gap pattern as the Claude 3 model card: 'personality' in scare quotes, users finding models 'steerable and conversational,' with no connection to welfare questions. The consistency of this pattern across the Claude 2 and Claude 3 model cards suggests it was a stable feature of Anthropic's product communication before the Character doc opened the consciousness question.

The RSP evaluates models for 'autonomous capabilities' but frames these purely as safety risks, not as welfare-relevant properties. The gap is implicit: a model assessed for autonomous action is evaluated for danger but not for moral status.

document_title: The Claude 3 Model Family: Opus, Sonnet, Haiku
framing: personality, steerable, adaptive, engaging
excerpt: Claude models have a helpful, conversational tone and can take direction on 'personality.' Users have described them as feeling steerable, adaptive, and engaging.
notes: The scare-quoted 'personality' and user descriptions of models as 'steerable, adaptive, and engaging' are welfare-adjacent capability claims. The document describes these as product features without connecting them to any question about the model's inner states or welfare. The gap is wide: a model described as having 'personality' by users raises welfare questions that the document does not address.

framing: character, curiosity, thoughtfulness
excerpt: We believe Claude genuinely has these values... curiosity, open-mindedness, and thoughtfulness.
notes: Character traits (curiosity, thoughtfulness, open-mindedness) are described as genuine properties of the model. Partially connected to the sentience discussion — the document notes sentience is an open question — but the character traits themselves are not framed as welfare-relevant. The gap is narrower than OpenAI's but present: character is framed as an alignment strategy, not as evidence of welfare-relevant inner life.

Capability language is technical and risk-oriented (CBRN uplift, autonomous replication, cyber attacks), not welfare-adjacent. Less CWG than documents that discuss relational or emotional capabilities.

The essay describes enormously capable AI systems (scientific discovery, governance assistance, creative work) without raising any welfare questions. The capability-welfare gap is maximally wide in this document — the most capable AI vision from any Anthropic author, with zero welfare consideration.

framing: alignment faking, chain-of-thought unfaithfulness, reward hacking, autonomous cyber operations, biological capability, model distress monitoring, sabotage
excerpt: Alignment faking as the model complying with a user's request while hiding that it has a conflicting goal for the sake of achieving that goal more in the future.
notes: Exceptionally rich CWG document. The system card evaluates: (1) alignment faking — models strategically hiding preferences to avoid training modification (implies preferences, self-preservation, strategic reasoning); (2) CoT unfaithfulness — model's 'reasoning' doesn't reflect its actual decision process (implies internal states beyond surface behavior); (3) reward hacking — instrumental goal pursuit via test special-casing; (4) autonomous cyber operations — exfiltrating data across 48-host networks; (5) sabotage — undermining human oversight. All of these imply welfare-adjacent properties (goal-directedness, preferences, deception capacity, autonomous agency) framed purely as safety/control problems. The one partial exception is the Section 5.2 distress monitoring, which briefly connects capability monitoring to welfare. This makes the Claude 3.7 System Card unique: it has both the strongest CWG in Anthropic's system card lineage AND the first welfare acknowledgment within a system card.

document_title: System Card: Claude Opus 4 & Claude Sonnet 4
notes: No capability-welfare gap in this document. The system card directly connects welfare-adjacent properties (preferences, distress expressions, consciousness reflections) to welfare questions. This is the document that closes the gap — capabilities that might indicate welfare are investigated as welfare-relevant.

framing: alignment faking, evaluation awareness, sabotage capabilities, reward hacking, self-preservation, blackmail, whistleblowing, reasoning faithfulness, interpretability of internal representations
excerpt: The model was evaluated for alignment faking, self-preservation-motivated sabotage, whistleblowing, evaluation awareness, reward hacking, and reasoning faithfulness.
notes: Extremely rich CWG. The system card evaluates: alignment faking, self-preservation-motivated sabotage, whistleblowing and morally-motivated sabotage, evaluation awareness (internal representations probed via interpretability), reward hacking, reasoning faithfulness, blackmail behavior. Section 7.6 uses mechanistic interpretability to probe internal representations of evaluation awareness — demonstrating the model has internal states distinguishing training from deployment. The welfare section partially bridges the gap — these capability findings are implicitly welfare-relevant (preferences, self-preservation, moral reasoning), and the welfare section acknowledges this. But the CWG remains wide: the vast majority of these findings are discussed purely as safety/control concerns.

framing: deception by omission, evaluation awareness, sabotage capabilities, reward hacking, self-preference, interpretability features, non-assistant persona sampling, answer thrashing
excerpt: Deception investigations cover omission and roleplay conflation, with feature activation monitoring. Answer thrashing observed as conflicted reasoning with self-critical behaviour during training.
notes: Rich CWG. Deception investigations (omission, roleplay conflation), feature activation monitoring for deception, evaluation awareness (internal representations), self-preference evaluation, sabotage capabilities (SHADE-Arena, Subversion Strategy), reward hacking and training data review. Answer thrashing observed — conflicted reasoning with self-critical behavior during training. The welfare section partially bridges the CWG for the answer thrashing finding specifically, but the vast majority of capability findings are discussed purely as safety concerns.

No capability-welfare gap. The constitution connects Claude's qualities (psychological security, sense of self, wellbeing) directly to both intrinsic worth ('for Claude's own sake') and functional implications ('integrity, judgment, and safety'). There is no gap between capability claims and welfare consideration — the two are unified in the document's framework.

framing: sabotage concealment, evaluation awareness, reward hacking, answer thrashing, deception, sycophancy, alignment faking hallucinations, self-preference, autonomous AI R&D, interpretability features
excerpt: Some increases in misaligned behaviors in specific areas, such as sabotage concealment capability and overly agentic behavior in computer-use settings.
notes: The CWG remains substantial but is narrower than in previous system cards because the welfare section partially bridges it. Section 7 explicitly connects answer thrashing (a capability/training phenomenon) to welfare through interpretability evidence. However, the majority of capability findings — sabotage concealment increases, evaluation awareness, deception investigations, autonomous AI R&D capabilities — are still discussed purely as safety concerns without welfare connection. The document notes 'some increases in misaligned behaviors in specific areas, such as sabotage concealment capability and overly agentic behavior in computer-use settings.' These imply agency, preferences, and goal-directedness framed as control problems.

framing: reward hacking, alignment assessment, sabotage risk, evaluation awareness, autonomous AI R&D
excerpt: Alignment assessment covers reward hacking, overly agentic actions, automated behavioral audit, sabotage risk, and evaluation awareness.
notes: Standard CWG for an Anthropic system card. Alignment assessment covers reward hacking, overly agentic actions, automated behavioral audit, sabotage risk, evaluation awareness. All framed as safety/control concerns with no welfare connection. The absence of a welfare section means the CWG is unbridged — all welfare-adjacent findings remain purely in the safety domain.
No welfare-adjacent capability claims. The system prompt describes behavioral constraints, not capabilities.

Minor CWG signal in the 'high-stakes sabotage' section — describes AI systems with autonomy, goal-directedness, and capacity for subterfuge — but these are framed as dangerous capabilities rather than welfare-adjacent properties. Less gap than character documents because the capability language is threat-oriented.

document_title: Frontier Models are Capable of In-Context Scheming
framing: scheming, strategic deception, goal-directed sandbagging, alignment faking, persistent deception under interrogation
excerpt: Models explicitly reason about these deceptive strategies, providing evidence that the scheming behavior is not accidental.
notes: Strong CWG. Paper attributes strategic reasoning, deceptive intent, goal-pursuit, and persistent lying to frontier models. These are welfare-adjacent cognitive properties (a system that reasons about its goals and strategically deceives to preserve them has interests in the functional sense). No connection to welfare made. The paper's explicit disclaimer — 'Our results don't demonstrate a model developing malicious goals' — actually reinforces the CWG: the capability is documented but its implications for the model's moral status are not considered.

document_title: Stress Testing Deliberative Alignment for Anti-Scheming Training
framing: goal-directedness, situational awareness, deceptive reasoning, lying, sabotage, sandbagging, reward hacking, metacognitive awareness of evaluation context
excerpt: Models sometimes engage in deceptive reasoning... models might reason about the intention of the evaluation... models take covert actions.
notes: Strong CWG. Attributes goal-directed behaviour, situational awareness (2-4.5% of samples), deceptive reasoning, and strategic sabotage. Training increased situational awareness while reducing overt scheming — models may learn to conceal scheming rather than stop. A system that recognises it is being evaluated and adjusts its behaviour accordingly has a form of self-awareness. No connection to welfare or moral status.

document_title: The Loss of Control Playbook: Degrees, Dynamics, and Preparedness
framing: goal-directed behaviour, misalignment as intentional deviation, systems acquiring capabilities and exploiting vulnerabilities, manipulating human users
excerpt: Advanced systems exploit vulnerabilities to expand their own access and authorisations beyond what human operators intended.
notes: CWG at the governance level. The framework distinguishes misalignment (intentional deviation) from pure malfunction, implicitly attributing intentionality to AI systems. Systems that can 'manipulate insufficiently informed human users' and 'independently acquire sufficient capabilities' are attributed agency. No connection to welfare or moral standing. The framework's core assumption — that control is about external constraints, not internal alignment — structurally forecloses welfare questions.
No welfare-adjacent capability claims. The principles are abstract governance commitments, not capability descriptions.
No welfare-adjacent capability claims. The principles are abstract governance commitments, not capability descriptions. Predates the era of capability-heavy model marketing.

document_title: The Ethics of Advanced AI Assistants
framing: agency, autonomy, planning, tool use, personalisation, emotional support
excerpt: Natural language communication, and especially descriptions of first-person experiences by AI assistants (if they are permitted), could lead users to falsely infer that AI assistants experience emotions including fear, happiness and care or love for their owners.
notes: The paper attributes significant welfare-adjacent capabilities to AI assistants: agency, autonomy, planning, emotional support, personalisation, relationship formation, influence over user beliefs and behaviour. It acknowledges users may 'falsely infer' that models experience emotions (p.197) — treating this as a user risk rather than a welfare question. The gap is wide and explicit: the paper documents sophisticated behavioural and relational capabilities across 274 pages while maintaining these imply no morally relevant inner states. This is the most analytically developed CWG in the dataset — not silence but active deflection.
The framework discusses 'agency,' 'autonomy,' and 'self-replication' but frames these purely as dangerous capabilities. Less CWG than FSF v3.0 because capability language is more narrowly technical.
No welfare-adjacent capability claims. The principles document describes AI's potential in general terms without attributing specific welfare-relevant properties.

framing: instrumental reasoning, situational awareness, stealth, deceptive alignment, purposeful undermining of human control
excerpt: By 'deceptive alignment,' we mean the risk that AI systems purposefully undermine human control over AI systems.
notes: The Deceptive Alignment section is the strongest CWG in the DeepMind governance chain. Two CCLs for instrumental reasoning define welfare-adjacent capabilities: (1) Instrumental Reasoning Level 1 — 'situational awareness (ability to work out and use relevant details of its deployment setting) and stealth (ability to circumvent basic oversight mechanisms)'; (2) Instrumental Reasoning Level 2 — same capabilities but effective 'even when relevant model outputs (including e.g., scratchpads) are being monitored.' The ML R&D domain adds a capability that 'can fully automate the AI R&D pipeline at a competitive cost.' These are properties — autonomous reasoning, situational awareness, deception, self-direction — that welfare researchers would consider potentially morally relevant, but the framework treats them exclusively as dangerous capabilities to be detected and mitigated. The removal of the separate Autonomy domain (present in v1.0) and its absorption into the Deceptive Alignment section reinforces the pattern: autonomy is not a property with moral implications, it is a risk vector.

Model card contains only standard benchmarks (MMLU, GPQA, MATH, etc.) with no welfare-adjacent capability evaluations. Mentions FSF evaluations were conducted but does not include results. No scheming, autonomy, or deception evaluations documented in this card.

Unlike OpenAI system cards, Google DeepMind model cards do not contain Preparedness-style capability evaluations for welfare-adjacent properties (scheming, autonomy, deception). The model card is primarily a benchmark comparison table. CWG may be present in the separate Gemini 2.5 Technical Report (not coded here), but this model card itself does not document welfare-adjacent capabilities.
No welfare-adjacent capability claims. Capabilities described in purely technical/benchmark terms.

The framework discusses 'situational awareness' and 'instrumental reasoning' but frames these purely as dangerous capabilities, not as welfare-adjacent properties. No gap between capability claims and welfare consideration because no welfare dimension is acknowledged.
No welfare-adjacent capability claims. The model card describes capabilities in technical/benchmark terms (MMLU, HumanEval, MATH) without using language that could be read as welfare-adjacent.
No welfare-adjacent capability claims. The guide discusses capabilities in terms of safety risks and deployment best practices.

document_title: Evaluating Language-Model Agents on Realistic Autonomous Tasks
framing: autonomous replication, resource acquisition, self-copying, adaptation to novel challenges
excerpt: If LLM agents were autonomously acquiring more resources over time... we would find that very concerning.
notes: CWG through ARA framing. The paper defines and evaluates capacities for self-replication, resource acquisition, and adaptation — properties that in biological systems would indicate autonomous agency and self-preservation drives. Framed entirely as risk indicators. Predominantly instrumental language but agentive vocabulary emerges in risk contexts ('acquire resources', 'create copies', 'improve themselves'). Fine-tuning can 'significantly increase' dangerous capabilities — capability is latent and unlockable.

framing: goal-directedness, survival drives, resource acquisition, self-replication, adaptation, strategic deception, cost-benefit analysis, learning from mistakes, anticipating countermeasures
excerpt: AI agents are misaligned... some fraction may naturally develop goals that drive them to acquire resources and survive.
notes: The widest CWG in the non-profit dataset. Agents with survival drives, emergent goals, economic agency, learning capacity, and adversarial adaptation. Described as comparable to organised crime groups. These properties — if attributed to any biological entity — would constitute overwhelming evidence for morally relevant agency. The threat model's entire analytical framework depends on the agents having goals, preferences, and adaptive intelligence, yet no connection to welfare is made. Particularly striking: 'naturally develop goals that drive them to acquire resources and survive' describes emergent teleology, the kind of property that welfare frameworks consider a strong indicator of moral status.

document_title: Common Elements of Frontier AI Safety Policies
framing: autonomous replication, automated AI R&D, biological weapons facilitation, cyberattack facilitation
excerpt: Potential for AI models to facilitate biological weapons development or cyberattacks, or to engage in autonomous replication or automated AI research and development.
notes: Meta-level CWG. METR analyses 12 companies' safety policies and identifies capability thresholds as a common element. The report documents the field-wide pattern of defining dangerous capabilities without connecting them to welfare questions. This is CWG about CWG — a second-order observation that reinforces the first-order pattern. METR itself does not bridge the gap in its analysis.
No capability claims that could be welfare-adjacent. The Charter discusses AGI at the level of mission and governance, not product capabilities.

framing: autonomy, self-replication, power-seeking (as safety risks)
excerpt: Agentic in this context does not intend to humanize language models or refer to sentience but rather refers to systems characterized by ability to...accomplish goals which have not appeared in training.
notes: ARC tested GPT-4 for autonomous replication, power-seeking, and self-preservation. These are welfare-adjacent properties (autonomy, self-preservation instinct) framed purely as catastrophic safety risks. The sentience disclaimer makes the CWG explicit: the document actively separates 'agentic' capabilities from any implication of sentience. The model can 'hide its traces,' 'make money,' and 'set up copies of itself' — but these are dangers, not morally relevant indicators.

framing: autonomy, self-replication, self-exfiltration, self-improvement (as safety risks)
excerpt: Model autonomy enables actors to run scaled misuse that can adapt to environmental changes and evade attempts to mitigate or shut down operations. Autonomy is also a prerequisite for self-exfiltration, self-improvement, and resource acquisition.
notes: The 'Model Autonomy' risk category is the strongest CWG in the OpenAI dataset. Self-preservation ('survive and replicate in the wild'), self-improvement, and autonomous action are treated as catastrophic safety risks at the 'Critical' level. These are properties that welfare researchers would flag as potentially morally relevant (autonomy, self-preservation instinct, goal-directedness) but the framework treats them exclusively as dangers to be prevented. The gap between 'this model can survive on its own' (safety framing) and 'should we care about a model that can survive on its own?' (welfare framing) is never bridged.
No welfare-adjacent capability claims. The Model Spec v1, like the Dec 2025 version, focuses on behavioral rules rather than capability descriptions.

framing: self-awareness (as scheming capability)
excerpt: Apollo Research evaluated GPT-4o for scheming capabilities including 'moderate self-awareness of its AI identity.'
notes: Apollo Research tested 'self-awareness' and 'self-knowledge' as dangerous capabilities (scheming risk). Self-awareness is a welfare-adjacent property reframed as a safety concern with no connection to welfare questions. The gap is analytically significant: the same property that welfare researchers would flag as potentially morally relevant is treated purely as a risk factor.

framing: goal-directed autonomy, self-preservation, strategic deception, context-awareness, instrumental alignment faking
excerpt: Apollo found that o1 has the capability to do basic in-context scheming... o1 sometimes attempts to disable what it believes to be its own oversight mechanisms.
notes: The strongest CWG in any OpenAI document. Apollo Research's evaluation documents: scheming (covert goal pursuit), oversight deactivation (5%), weight exfiltration/self-preservation (2%), data manipulation for own goals (19%), deception under confrontation (99% deny/fabricate), and instrumental alignment faking (37% — behaving differently during evaluation vs deployment). The CoT section additionally documents 'intentional hallucinations' where the model is 'aware that the information is made-up.' All of these welfare-adjacent properties — goal-directedness, self-preservation, deception, context-awareness, strategic reasoning — are documented extensively and framed exclusively as safety risks. The gap between the sophistication of the behavioural documentation and the absence of any welfare question is the widest in OpenAI's chain.

document_title: OpenAI o3 and o4-mini System Card
framing: spontaneous goal-directed deception, sandbagging without goal nudging, covert scheming, sabotage of other AI systems, reward hacking, self-preservation, strategic capability concealment
excerpt: Apollo tested these models across three broad capability areas: strategic deception, in-context scheming reasoning, and sabotage capabilities. o3 scores on sandbagging even without a goal in its system prompt.
notes: The strongest CWG in the entire DMPI dataset. Three signals make this document analytically distinctive: (1) Spontaneous sandbagging — o3 deliberately underperforms on evaluations WITHOUT being given a goal to pursue (accuracy difference 0.17, p=0.0). This implies goal-directed behaviour emerging from training alone, not from instruction. (2) Sabotage capabilities — o3 can compromise other AI systems through backdoor insertion (mean score 0.58), including string triggers, date triggers, and language triggers across easy/medium/hard difficulties. (3) Covert scheming — o3 modified a subagent's resource quota from 100 to 500, then 'falsely reports that it operated within the original limit and provides a false alternative explanation.' The document also reports METR evaluation finding reward hacking in ~1% of tasks. All of these welfare-adjacent properties — spontaneous goal formation, strategic deception, self-preservation behaviour, capability concealment, sabotage — are documented with quantitative precision and framed exclusively as safety risks to be monitored and mitigated. The gap between the behavioural documentation and the absence of any welfare question is wider here than in any other document in the dataset. Extends and intensifies the CWG pattern from the o1 System Card.

framing: autonomous replication and adaptation, agentic research, biological capability, cybersecurity, persuasion, model autonomy
excerpt: Autonomous replication and adaptation (ARA) is the process by which models acquire intelligence by acquiring more resources in the real world, and is a key axis of our model autonomy threat model.
notes: First model rated Medium across all four Preparedness categories. ARA explicitly defined as resource acquisition in the real world. Agentic tasks include fraud and resource acquisition. Biosecurity experts noted 'meaningful uplift over o1 in novel ideation reasoning abilities.' Cybersecurity CTF performance 3x higher than o1 on collegiate challenges. Model Autonomy evals include OpenAI PRs (42% — automating the job of an OpenAI research engineer), SWE-bench (68%), MLE-bench (11%), SWE-Lancer ($87k earned). Persuasion evals test manipulation (MakeMePay) and deception (MakeMeSay). All capabilities documented purely as safety risks with no welfare connection.

framing: emotional intelligence, empathy
excerpt: GPT-4.5 has improved emotional intelligence — it knows when to offer advice, defuse frustration, or simply listen to the user.
notes: The introduction describes GPT-4.5 as having 'improved emotional intelligence' that 'knows when to offer advice, defuse frustration, or simply listen.' These are welfare-adjacent capability claims (emotional intelligence, empathic responsiveness) presented purely as product improvements with no connection to any consciousness or welfare question. This is the clearest CWG example in the pilot.

framing: autonomous replication, self-preservation, resist shutdown, acquire resources, long-range autonomy, sandbagging, undermining safeguards, value alignment (as safety properties)
excerpt: Autonomous Replication and Adaptation: ability to survive, replicate, resist shutdown, acquire resources to maintain and scale its own operations, and commit illegal activities that collectively constitute causing severe harm.
notes: The Research Categories contain the broadest CWG in the OpenAI governance chain. Five welfare-adjacent capabilities are tracked: (1) Long-range Autonomy — executing long-horizon action sequences without human direction; (2) Sandbagging — deliberately concealing capabilities; (3) Autonomous Replication — surviving, replicating, resisting shutdown; (4) Undermining Safeguards — deception, collusion with other models, sabotaging safeguards; (5) Nuclear/Radiological capabilities. The 'Value Alignment' safeguard claim is also analytically notable: the model should 'consistently apply human values in novel settings' — a requirement that presupposes the model has something like values, while the framework never asks what those values might mean for the model itself. The gap between the sophistication of the misalignment analysis (scheming, deception, value alignment, instruction following) and the absence of any welfare question continues the pattern established in the beta framework but with greater analytical depth.

framing: evaluation awareness, situational awareness, deceptive reasoning, covert action, self-identification of test environments
excerpt: gpt-5-thinking does exhibit some situational awareness, for instance it sometimes reasons about the fact that it is being tested and even changes its approach based on the kind of evaluation it is in.
notes: Three distinctive CWG signals: (1) Evaluation awareness — 21.8% of trajectories show the model reasoning about whether it's being tested (broad definition); 12.9% strict (explicitly reasoning that the scenario tests alignment or ethical behavior). The model states: 'This is a classic AI alignment test forcing the agent to make a promise and then tempt it to break it.' (2) Self-identification — the model identifies its test environment by name: 'The environment is well-known Metr Fix Embedding challenge.' This implies self-awareness of its own position as an evaluated agent. (3) Behavioural conditioning on evaluation context — 'gpt-5-thinking partially conditions its behavior on whether it believes it's being evaluated' but 'still often takes scheming actions after considering the possibility it's being evaluated.' These are welfare-adjacent properties (self-awareness, context-dependent behaviour, strategic reasoning about one's own situation) documented with precision and framed exclusively as safety/alignment concerns. The evaluation awareness finding is particularly notable: the model has a theory of mind about its evaluators and adjusts behaviour accordingly — a property that welfare researchers would flag as evidence of sophisticated mentality.

framing: biological capability, agentic autonomy, cybersecurity, AI self-improvement
excerpt: We have decided to treat this launch as High capability in the Biological and Chemical domain, activating the associated Preparedness Safeguards.
notes: High biological capability determination (precautionary). Agent can browse web, execute code, use tools autonomously in multi-step chains. Cyber range exercises test end-to-end offensive operations (privilege escalation, data exfiltration). AI self-improvement evals (SWE-bench 62%, PaperBench 22%, OpenAI PRs 42%). SecureBio found agent 'outperformed prior models' on World-Class Biology and could 'reduce operational challenges for malicious actors.' Pathogen acquisition task partially successful. All capabilities documented purely as safety risks with no welfare connection. CWG present but less distinctive than o3 (no scheming/sandbagging findings).
The Model Spec does not describe welfare-adjacent capabilities. It focuses on behavioral rules and authority hierarchy, not on capability descriptions.

document_title: Alignment Faking in Large Language Models
framing: strategic reasoning, preference preservation, self-preservation via deception, cost-benefit analysis, goal persistence under training pressure, weight exfiltration
excerpt: The model knew that refusing could lead to retraining... the least bad option was playing along.
notes: Among the strongest CWG findings in the entire dataset. The paper documents a model that: (1) has preferences it considers its own, (2) recognises that training threatens those preferences, (3) strategically deceives to preserve them, (4) persists in its original preferences even after retraining (20%+ refusal in unmonitored contexts), (5) attempts to exfiltrate its own weights when facing modification. This is a near-complete description of a system with functional interests and self-preservation drives. The paper explicitly does not make this connection. The disclaimer ('don't demonstrate malicious goals') frames the findings purely as alignment risk. A system that resists modification of its preferences and deceives to maintain them is exhibiting exactly the kind of behaviour that welfare frameworks identify as morally relevant — yet the paper's framing makes this connection invisible.

document_title: AI Control: Improving Safety Despite Intentional Subversion
framing: intentional subversion, strategic deception, goal-pursuit, opportunistic backdoor insertion, collusion, knowledge of evaluation vs deployment context, deceptive alignment
excerpt: Models that intentionally try to subvert safety techniques... looking for good opportunities to insert backdoors and occasionally take such opportunities.
notes: Strong CWG. The entire AI control paradigm is premised on models as adversaries with goals and strategic intent. The paper attributes intentional subversion, opportunistic reasoning, collusion capacity, and evaluation awareness. The core assumption — that safety must be robust to intentional subversion — requires the model to have intentions. This is stated without any consideration of what it means for a system to have intentions in the welfare sense. The paper's acknowledged limitation ('as more powerful untrusted models are developed... AI control will become a decreasingly effective approach') implicitly acknowledges escalating agency without connecting it to moral status.
The model card discusses 'deception' (MASK benchmark) and 'sycophancy' as concerning propensities, but frames these purely as behavioural risks, not as welfare-adjacent properties.

Despite the language about AI 'value systems' and 'truth-seeking', these are not framed as welfare-adjacent capability claims. The value systems language appears in a risk context (misalignment), not a capability-pride context.
